The cannon is cast at Stony Hill in Florida after the ore is "smelted in the great furnaces of Goldspring" and shipped south by "no less than sixty-eight vessels of 1,000 tons" (FEM-015-006). The pour requires "1,200 furnaces," each holding "about 1,400,000 lbs. of metal" and "built on the model of those used for the casting of the Rodman gun" (FEM-015-007). At noon on casting day "the cannon was suddenly fired" as a signal and "twelve hundred tapping-holes were opened simultaneously," sending the molten iron plunging "down a depth of 900 feet" (FEM-015-022). The spectacle throws artificial clouds "to a height of 3,000 feet into the air," as though "a new crater in the heart of Florida" had opened (FEM-015-022).
